elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we investigate the compactness and the long time behavior of killed Feynman-Kac semigroups of various processes arising from statistical physics with very general singular Schrödinger potentials.
elements.sent-2411-005: The processes we consider cover a large class of processes used in statistical physics, with strong links with quantum mechanics and (local or not) Schrödinger operators (including e.g. fractional Laplacians).
elements.sent-2411-006: For instance we consider solutions to elliptic differential equations, Lévy processes, the kinetic Langevin process with locally Lipschitz gradient fields, and systems of interacting Lévy particles.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our analysis relies on a Perron-Frobenius type theorem derived in a previous work [A.. Guillin, B.
elements.sent-2411-009: Soc.] for Feller kernels and on the tools introduced in [L.. Wu, 2004, Probab.
elements.sent-2411-010: Fields] to compute bounds on the essential spectral radius of a bounded nonnegative kernel.
elements.sent-2411-011: Feynman-Kac, singular potentials, Schrödinger, quasi-stationary distributions, Brownian particle, Lévy processes, kinetic Langevin.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Setting and purpose of this work
elements.sent-2411-012: The purpose of this work is to study the basic properties and above all, the long time behavior of killed Feynman-Kac semigroups of several models with very general singular Schrödinger potentials (see Section \ref{sec.Sc and more precisely [S1], [S2], and \eqref{Eq.Ui ).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The killing occurs when the process exists a (position) subdomain\footnote{A domain is by definition a non-empty, open, and connected set.. .}
elements.sent-2411-014: We have no assumption on the regularity of the boundary of this subdomain which can be bounded or not and whose boundary can intersect the points where the Schrödinger potential is infinite (which is the case of interest).
elements.sent-2411-015: For the first three considered processes, the singular potential arises from fixed a point located at $0$ whereas for the fourth one, it is created by $n$ interacting Lévy particles (see the fourth model below in Section~\ref{sec.IVB ).}
elements.sent-2411-016: As explained in Section~\ref{sec.Ss , more general singular potentials can be considered.}
elements.sent-2411-017: This work is also motivated by the strong link between Feynman-Kac semigroups and solution of evolution equations associated with a Schrödinger type operator, see~\eqref{Eq.Sch .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The introduction and the paper are organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-019: We first introduce the class of singular Schrödinger potentials we will treat in details in this work and we then give the notation which will be used throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-020: Next, we introduce the four models and we give preliminary results on their behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-021: These are the purposes of Section~\ref{sec.FK .}
